coredata.dc:title: pH-induced synergistic changes in color and shape of soft actuator based on degradable carbon dots/sodium alginate gel
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yingge
Yang, Xuyuan
Zhang, Yongqiang
Lu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: Soft actuators for intelligent robots require further elaboration to improve their biomedical applicability, which has led to the development of a series of flexible stimulus-responsive materials. However, fabricating degradable soft actuators that exhibit synergistic color and shape changes in response to environmental stimuli remains challenging. Here, we developed a soft actuating gel based on carbon dots (CDs) that are chemically cross-linked with sodium alginate. The soft actuating gel exhibits rapid synergistic changes in color and shape in response to pH owing to CDs protonation and deprotonation. Soft actuators constructed using the gel and poly (lactic acid) tape not only perform actions, such as grasping and lifting, but also camouflage and warn through self-color changes. The combination of pH-responsive CDs and degradable polymers provides a simple strategy for fabricating degradable soft actuators that exhibit synergistic changes in color and shape, and is expected to promote further research into soft actuators.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Soft actuator
Degradable
pH-responsive
Carbon dots
Sodium alginate
Color-change

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH-induced color and shape change of CDs-SA actuator
CDs-SA robot and the conceptional lifeguard

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation and characterization of CDs. (a) Illustration of the preparation of CDs. (b) TEM images of CDs at pH 3. (c) The size distribution of CDs at pH 3, 5 and 7. (d) The FL images of CDs at different pH. (e) The FL spectrum of CDs at different pH. (f) The emission wavelength of CDs at different pH. (g) The UV–Vis NIR spectra of CDs at different pH. (h) The Zeta potential of CDs at different pH. Abbreviations: CDs, carbon dots.

Synthesis and characterization of CDs-SA gel. (a) 3D model of CDs-SA gel. (b) SEM image of CDs-SA gel. (c) Synthesis of CDs-SA gel. (d) FT-IR spectra of CDs, SA and CDs-SA gel. (e) FT-IR spectra of the enlarged area, 2000 cm−1 to 1000 cm−1. (f) The photos of folded and tangled CDs-SA gel. (g) The transmittance spectra and photos of CDs-SA gel with different content of CDs, 0.01 %, 0.02 % and 0.03 %, w/v. (h) The FL spectra and photos of CDs-SA gel at different pH. (i) The UV–Vis spectra of CDs-SA gel at different pH. Abbreviations: CDs-SA gel, carbon dots-sodium alginate gel.

The preparation and characterization of CDs-SA actuator (CDs, 0.02 %, w/v). (a) The photo, 3D model and SEM image of the CDs-SA actuator. (b) The illustrated deformation of the CDs-SA actuator. (c) The deformation of CDs-SA actuator under different pH in 20 s. (d) The deformation process of CDs-SA actuator at pH 5. (e) The color change of CDs-SA actuator under different pH.
(a) The mechanism of pH-induced deformation of CDs-SA gel. (b) The SEM images of different pH CDs-SA gel.

Application of CDs-SA actuator (CDs, 0.02 %, w/v). (a) Schematic illustration of the grasping robot for grasping and lifting a plastic block. (b) Grasping and lifting photos of the grasping robot under pH stimulation. (c) Schematic illustration of the conceptional lifeguard. (d) Lifting photos of the conceptional lifeguard.

head.ce:title: pH-induced synergistic changes in color and shape of soft actuator based on degradable carbon dots/sodium alginate gel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Institute of Advanced Technology, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450052, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Institute of Advanced Technology, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450052, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Soft actuators for intelligent robots require further elaboration to improve their biomedical applicability, which has led to the development of a series of flexible stimulus-responsive materials. However, fabricating degradable soft actuators that exhibit synergistic color and shape changes in response to environmental stimuli remains challenging. Here, we developed a soft actuating gel based on carbon dots (CDs) that are chemically cross-linked with sodium alginate. The soft actuating gel exhibits rapid synergistic changes in color and shape in response to pH owing to CDs protonation and deprotonation. Soft actuators constructed using the gel and poly (lactic acid) tape not only perform actions, such as grasping and lifting, but also camouflage and warn through self-color changes. The combination of pH-responsive CDs and degradable polymers provides a simple strategy for fabricating degradable soft actuators that exhibit synergistic changes in color and shape, and is expected to promote further research into soft actuators.

A degradable soft actuating gel based on carbon dots (CDs) cross-linked with sodium alginate exhibits rapid synergistic changes in color and shape in response to pH owing to CDs protonation and deprotonation. The gel can be composited with other material to construct degradable dual-functional soft actuators.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The high demand for intelligent robots in biomedicine has stimulated the development of soft actuators (Shi et al., 2019). In contrast to rigid actuators, soft actuators composed of flexible materials are lighter and softer; consequently, they are more adaptable to organisms (Pal et al., 2021). Benefiting from flexible materials, soft actuators are capable of performing more-complex and systematic movements, particularly for simulating biological functions. Therefore, soft actuators are broadly used as artificial muscles, to replace damaged tissue, as carriers for targeted drug delivery (Liu, Huang, et al., 2024), in minimally invasive surgery (Sambyal et al., 2023), and to manipulate cells (De Pascali et al., 2022). Various materials have been developed for soft actuators, including liquid-crystal polymers (Liu, Fang, et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2024), shape-memory polymers (Wu et al., 2022), gels (Na et al., 2022), and fluids (Apsite et al., 2022). Soft actuators convert energy by exploiting the responsive properties of materials to external light (Chen et al., 2022), electric fields, magnetic fields (Sambyal et al., 2023), heat (Hu et al., 2023), ions (Zeng et al., 2023), or humidity (Oh et al., 2024), and change shape or move through bilayer, directional, or gradated structures. As applications expand, single-functional soft actuators are no longer able meet diverse needs. Hence, there is a strong need for the development of multi-functional soft actuators. Among them, color-change synergistic soft actuators that integrate sensing and actuation are clearly advantageous and have attracted wide attention because they simplify closed-loop monitoring, provide real-time environmental feedback, and are strongly environmentally adaptable (Nie et al., 2021).

In nature, chameleons and octopuses change the colors and shapes of their bodies to camouflage, hunt, evade predators, and communicate, whose color changes usually resulted from the aggregation/dispersion of pigment-containing organelles and structural color effects (Teyssier et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2022). Inspired by these biological phenomena, scientists have explored various color-tunable flexible materials for use in soft actuators. Some soft actuators undergo color and shape changes in response to the same stimulus, where color change and shape change are two unrelated processes, as exemplified by the combined thermally induced phase transition of the liquid crystal and its thermally induced color change of the tetrarylbutadionitrile radical (Liu et al., 2021), as well as the combined thermally induced phase transitions of the liquid crystal and the color change of the microgel (Zhong et al., 2022). Some studies have also shown that color can be affected by the shape of the soft actuator. Chen et al. prepared electrically inducible hydrogel actuators containing luminophores that exhibit aggregation-induced emission (AIE), which act through electrically promoted dehydration and AIE (Xie et al., 2023). Chen et al. also prepared a thermosensitive organic hydrogel actuator doped with Eu+ ions. The organic hydrogel shrinks when exposed to NIR light or heat, with Eu+ ions bonding to the ligands to afford a blue-to-red change in the fluorescence of the hydrogel (Shang et al., 2022). The color and shape changes exhibited by some soft actuators are ascribable to single components, with examples including a polymer gel actuator crosslinked with hydrophobic carbon dots (CDs) that exhibit optical AIE properties (Wu et al., 2021). Because the CDs gather in water, the polymer network is deformed, and the gel undergoes a blue-to-red color change. The development of new materials and designs that deliver color change and actuation using a single material in response to a single stimulus is of great significance for reducing manufacturing difficulties and improving the synergistic abilities of soft actuators.

The accumulation of waste must also be considered owing to the rapid development of soft-actuator technology. In this regard, sustainable materials and transient systems are promising and may provide sustainable soft-actuator technologies (Rumley et al., 2023). Synthetic polymers, such as poly (lactic acid) (PLA), poly (hydroxyalkanoate)s, and poly (caprolactone) are common biodegradable polymeric materials (Oh et al., 2024). Tunable PLA actuators doped with ions have been developed with the aim of changing PLA crystallinity, and magnetically driven polypropylene carbonate soft actuators have also been reported (M. Correia et al., 2020). Natural products and their derivatives, such as gelatin (Baumgartner et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2023), polysaccharides (Hu et al., 2023), and silk proteins (Deng et al., 2022), have also been used to prepare biodegradable soft actuators. Sodium alginate (SA) is a natural polysaccharide that contains biodegradable glycosidic bonds, and is a promising option for use in biomedical soft actuators owing to its biocompatibility and adhesiveness. Gradated nanoporous composite hydrogel actuators based on SA and chitosan (Cui et al., 2019), biomass-based soft actuators formed by SA and epoxidized natural rubber (Zhu et al., 2020), and multi-stimulus-responsive actuators composed of SA and polydimethylsiloxane have been studied (Sun et al., 2024).

With the above background in mind, we chose to prepare a soft actuator using SA, and hypothesized that a simple CDs-crosslinked SA strategy can be used to prepare biodegradable soft actuators capable of grasping and lifting in a stimulus-responsive manner. CDs are cost-effective zero-dimensional photoluminescent carbon nanomaterials with abundant and adjustable surface functional groups and a broad range of sources (Wang et al., 2023; Zhang & Lu, 2024). CDs derived from 3,4-dihydroxyaniline are photoluminescent and pH-responsive. The introduction of CDs derived from 3,4-dihydroxyaniline not only contributes to the construction of the three-dimensional gel network through amide and hydrogen bonds, but provides responsive units that realize actuator color and shape changes using a single material and a single stimulus. The design of an actuating layer based on CDs and SA helps to degrade and recycle the actuator at the end of its life. We show that a soft actuator fabricated using a CDs-SA actuating layer and a PLA passive layer readily grasps and lifts in response to pH. This study provides additional material and design possibilities for sustainable soft robots that cooperate multifunctionally in response to a single material exposed to a single stimulus, which helps reduce costs and manufacturing difficulties, while broadening the soft-actuator application range.

3,4-dihydroxyaniline hydrobromide, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Adamas. Sodium alginate (SA) was purchased from Aladdin (viscosity: 200 ± 20 mpa·s, G/M = 1:1, Mw: 98000 Da). Anhydrous ethanol (≥99 %) was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Ltd.

3,4-dihydroxyaniline hydrobromide was added to anhydrous ethanol to form a solution of 0.1 M. The solution was dispersed by sonication and then heated in an oven at 200 °C for 8 h. The prepared solution was obtained after centrifugation, filtration and dialysis. The dialyzed solution was frozen in a refrigerator and then frozen in a vacuum freeze dryer for 48 h to obtain CDs. The preparation was repeated five times.

SA was added to deionized water to form a 6 % (w/v) solution. Then, EDC was added to activate the carboxyl groups of SA. After 20 min, NHS was also added to the system. Among, the molar ration of carboxyl, EDC and NHS was 4:1:1. In 10 min, CDs were added to the above mixture to form solution (0.03 %, 0.02 %, 0.01 %, w/v). The reaction was kept for 12 h under stirring. After dialysis, the product was collected for next step. The preparation was repeated five times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-induced color and shape change of CDs-SA actuator
ce:section.ce:para: The configured gel was evenly coated on the PLA tape to form a gel/PLA bilayer. The gel/PLA bilayer was cut into the shape of “+”, placed in the solution (pH 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 and 13), and recorded the color-change and deformatio in 20s. The deformation process of the “+” bilayer with different CDs contents (0.01 %, 0.02 %, and 0.03 %, w/v) in pH 5 solution was also recorded at 5 s interval.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-SA robot and the conceptional lifeguard
ce:section.ce:para: The gel/PLA bilayer was cut into a “+” grasping clip. Then, the grasping clip was put in pH 5 solution. Meanwhile, the process of grasp and lift was recorded. The experiment was repeated five times.

The CDs-SA gel was cut into a rectangle (length: 6 cm, width: 1 cm). PLA tape was pasted on the front and back of the rectangle in order of every centimeter, which was named conceptional lifeguard. Place the conceptional lifeguard in pH 5 solution, then place a weight on the lifeguard. The process of change was observed and recorded. The experiment was also repeated five times.

Fluorescence (FL) spectra of CDs (0.1 mg/mL, pH 1/3/5/7/9/11/13, the range of detection wavelengths was 400–700 nm) and gels ((SA 6 % w/v, CDs 0.02 % w/v), pH 1/3/5/7/9/11/13, the range of detection wavelengths was 380–650 nm), and quantum yield of CDs were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, England) at room temperature, and each experiment was scanned three times. The FL spectra of CDs were measured on 1 and 11 days. The UV–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of CDs (0.1 mg/mL, pH 1/3/5/7/9/11/13) and CDs-SA gels ((SA 6 % w/v, CDs 0.02 % w/v), pH 1/3/5/7/9/11/13) were collected on a Shimadzu UV-3600i Plus spectrometer equipped with LabSolutions UV–Vis software, where the baseline was calibrated with water and Ba2CO3, respectively. The transmittance of the CDs-SA gel with different CDs contents (0.01 %, 0.02 %, and 0.03 %, w/v) was calculated from Lambert-Beer law (A = lg(1/T), A and T represent the absorbance and the transmittance, respectively) based on the UV–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra. Zeta potentials of CDs (1 mg/mL, pH 1/3/5/7/9/11/13) were obtained by nanoparticle size potential analyzer (Malvern Panaco zetasizer pro., Netherlands) equipped with ZS XPLORER software, and the mean value and the standard deviation were computed from Origin software based on the results of three repeated measurements. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs, SA, and CDs-SA gels were investigated by using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Bruker INVENIO R., Germany) equipped with OPUS 8.5 software, and the range of detection wavenumber was 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1. The FT-IR spectra of CDs were measured on 1 and 10 days. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs at pH 3, 7 and 11 were obtained using JEOL JEM-F200. Fifty CDs were randomly selected as samples whose size was measured by Nano Measurer1.2. Based on the size, the size-frequency distribution of CDs was calculated by Origin software. The lattice spacing of CDs was tested by Gatan Microscopy Suite software. Images of the gels ((SA 6 % w/v, CDs 0.02 % w/v), pH 3, 7 and 11) were obtained using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (ZEISS Sigma 300, Germany) at a voltage of 5.00KV. All the optical images were captured with a mobile phone. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of CDs was conducted on a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi surface analysis system at room temperature.

CDs were prepared from 3,4-dihydroxyaniline as the precursor and subjected to TEM, which revealed uniform quasi-spherical nanoparticles 1.4–2.8 nm in size with lattice spacings of around 0.23 nm (Fig. 1a, b and c). The sizes of the CDs were compared at pH 3, 7, and 11, which revealed that stable dispersive states were all maintained (Figs. 1c and S1). The XPS result suggested CDs are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and bromine atoms. The high-resolution XPS spectra revealed the presence of NH2 (Fig. S2). CDs (QY = 7.03 %) exhibited significant pH-responsive color changes when irradiated with UV light (Fig. 1d). The CDs solution was turquoise-green in color at pH 7, but gradually changed to yellow-green, yellow, and orange–yellow as the pH was lowered to 5, 3, and 1, respectively, and to yellow, yellow-green, and green as the pH was raised to 9, 11, and 13, respectively. The fluorescence-emission wavelength of the CDs was observed to redshift and weaken as the pH was decreased from 7 to 1, and a new emission peak appeared at 630 nm when irradiated at the same excitation wavelength (Fig. 1e and f). This observation is ascribable to protonation of the amino groups on the CDs surface, which alters the molecular state of the CDs, weakens the green emission, and triggers a red emission (Azad et al., 2021; Ehtesabi et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2022). The CDs emission wavelength was also observed to redshift and weaken slightly as the pH was increased from 7 to 13. These photoluminescence results are consistent with the fluorescence images (Fig. 1e and f). The UV–Vis absorption spectra reveal the presence of a new absorption peak at 400 nm at pH 5, 3, and 1 (Fig. 1g). Based on a comprehensive analysis of the FL and UV–Vis absorption spectra, we speculate that new ground-state compounds are formed that influence the optical behavior of the system (Wang & Lu, 2022). After 11 days, the FL spectrum of CDs at different pH were remeasured. No significant changes were observed in the emission wavelength except the CDs at pH 5, which revealed the stability of pH response (Fig. S3). The CDs are negatively charged at pH 7, and their zeta potential increases as the carboxyl group becomes ionized. In contrast, the CDs are positively charged when the amino groups are protonated (Fig. 1h).

We prepared a multifunctional actuator using a single material that responds to a single stimulus using CDs-SA gel as the actuating layer, which exploits the color-change and gel-swelling behavior promoted by CDs protonation and deprotonation (Fig. 2a and c). The FT-IR spectrum of the CDs shows dual amino peaks at 3145–3363 cm−1. After 10 days, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs was remeasured. No significant changes were observed, which revealed the stability of CDs (Fig. S4). A strong and wide absorption peak is observed at around 3423 cm−1, which is ascribable to associations formed by the hydroxyl groups of the SA. The peak at 3423 cm−1 is narrower following gelation owing to the lower degree of association between the hydroxyl groups of SA (Yin et al., 2021). A series of hydroxyl stretching-vibration peaks are observed near 2700 cm−1, which are ascribable to amino/carboxyl-group hydrogen bonding (Fig. 2d). Amide-bond formation led to a redshift in the carbonyl peak of SA, the disappearance of a symmetrical carboxyl-group stretching peak at 1415 cm−1, and the appearance of a CN absorption peak at approximately 1350 cm−1 (Fig. 2e). Taken together, these results indicate that the CDs and SA had formed chemical and physical crosslinks. The CDs and SA form a gel with a three-dimensional network structure owing to their amide and hydrogen-bond functionalities (Fig. 2b). The uniform pore structure of the CDs-SA gel confirmed that the CDs are evenly distributed in the gel, while the uniformly distributed CDs avoid uneven stress distribution in the actuating state and guarantee the structural shape change of the gel.

The CDs-SA gel is highly flexible and can be bent and twisted; it also exhibits good light transmittance, which decreases with increasing CDs content (Fig. 2f). The CDs-SA gels with 0.01 % and 0.02 % w/v CDs exhibited transmittances of over 97 % in the visible and infrared regions, while that of the gel with 0.03 % w/v CDs was approximately 50 %, consistent with a uniform and compact gel structure (Fig. 2g). Excited at the same excitation wavelength (395 nm), the emission wavelength of gels irrespective of pH was same (Fig. 2h). Thus, gelation clearly affects the CDs emission wavelength because the emission wavelength of the CDs solution is pH-dependent. This observation is possibly ascribable to the gel weakening the excitation energy reaching the CDs and the lower number of amino groups involved in protonation/deprotonation, which is insufficient to change the molecular state of the CDs and affect the emission wavelength. Gels showed significant changes in fluorescence intensity at various pH values, with that prepared at pH 5 fluorescing the strongest. The fluorescence intensity of the gel was observed to decrease rapidly with decreasing pH from 5 to 1; however, it initially decreased and then increased with increasing pH from 5 to 13. The CDs-SA gel appeared turquoise but with varying brightness under a UV lamp. The UV–Vis spectrum of the CDs-SA gel absorbed strongly at 200–300 and 325 nm owing to the π-π* transition of the sp2-hybridized core and the n-π* transition of CDs surface (Fig. 2i).

PLA tape was selected as the passive layer, and a pH-responsive soft actuator was prepared by compounding the CDs-SA gel with PLA using the coating method (Kumar et al., 2024). SEM revealed that a good interface was formed between the gel layer and the PLA tape (Fig. 3a). Active-to-passive-layer deformation occurred in response to pH (Fig. 3b). The CDs-SA soft actuator was cut into cruciforms and placed in solutions with different pH values. In 20 s, the CDs-SA actuator under pH 5 exhibited the largest deformation among the examined pH; the actuator gradually deformed less as the pH was decreased from 5 to 1. Similarly, the actuator became less deformed with increasing pH from 5 to 13 (Fig. 3c). Indeed, the actuator was not deformed at pH 11 or 1. Clearly, the actuating rate is controlled by pH. Amide bonds are intolerant to alkaline environments and prone to breakage. We speculate that the absence of gel deformation in a strongly alkaline environment is due to amide-bond breakage and the destruction of the three-dimensional network, which becomes incapable of creating the continuous stress required to drive actuator movement. Accordingly, the actuator can be used in the 1–9 pH range. The relationship between deformation and time was further investigated, which revealed that the CDs-SA actuator (CDs, 0.02 % w/v; pH 5) clearly deformed within 5 s; hence. it responds rapidly to pH (Fig. 3d). In contrast, the CD-SA actuator with the lower CDs content (CDs, 0.01 % w/v; pH 5) required 35 s to complete a similar deformation process, which is ascribable to its lower degree of crosslinking and weaker repulsion between its junctions (Fig. S5a). While the CD-SA actuator with the higher CDs content (CDs, 0.03 % w/v) required 15 s to complete the entire deformation process, it exhibited a lower degree of deformation than the other two actuators, which is possibly due to the high network density of the gel (Fig. S5b). Therefore, CD content affects the swelling kinetics of the gel and the motion of the actuator. When exposed to a UV lamp, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-SA actuator (CDs, 0.02 % w/v) gradually changed with pH (Figs. 3e and S6). The color change of CDs-SA gel was not as obvious as that of CDs. Thus, the color change may need portable device for detection.

The pH response of the CDs-SA actuator stems from the protonation/deprotonation of the carboxyl and amino groups in the three-dimensional network, which further influences the rearrangement and distribution of charge in the gel system. SA chains or CDs with the same charge are electrostatically repulsive, which expands and deforms the three-dimensional network. The amino groups of the CDs that do not form amide bonds become protonated and positively charged as the H+ concentration in the system is increased. The junction spacings in the gel increase owing to electrostatic repulsion between the CDs, which expands the three-dimensional network (Fig. 4a). SEM revealed that the CD-SA gel formed at pH 3 has larger pores than the that formed at pH 7. The carboxyl groups of SA become ionized upon the addition of OH− and exist as carboxylate ions as a consequence; hence, the SA chains repel each other. In addition, the amide bond is intolerant to strongly alkaline conditions. The SEM image of the CDs-SA gel formed at pH 11 shows a severely damaged gel network that is insufficiently strong to support CDs-SA-actuator deformation (Fig. 4b).

The CD-SA gels exhibited rapid bending behavior in response to pH; accordingly, we fabricated a grasping robot and conceptual lifeguard using CDs-SA gel and PLA tape and placed them in a pH-5 solution to examine their actuating properties (Fig. 5a and c). Gripping was observed in 10 s in response to pH. Moreover, the grasping robot wrapped, grabbed, and transported a plastic block in approximately 29 s (Fig. 5b and Table S1). The conceptual lifeguard lifted the analog in approximately 19 s, with lift-height easily adjusted by changing the composite structure of the PLA tape and CDs-SA gel (Fig. 5d and Table S1). The experiments of grasping robot and conceptual lifeguard were both repeated five times, whose results revealed the operability and stability of CDs-SA gel in soft actuator (Table S1).

We developed a CDs-SA gel that changes its fluorescence properties and shape, and was prepared by forming covalent and hydrogen bonds between CDs and SA. The protonation and deprotonation of CDs in response to pH affects the molecular state of the CDs surface and the cross-linking distance in the gel, which enables dual sensing and actuating with a single material using a single stimulation source. The degradable CDs-SA gel acts as an active layer and can be composited with other materials for the development of actuators that are environmentally friendly and sustainable. These advantages enabled the construction of a rapidly actuating grasping robot and conceptual lifeguard using the CDs-SA gel and PLA tape, thereby demonstrating that a simple CDs-crosslinked SA strategy can be used to prepare biodegradable soft actuators capable of grasping and lifting in a stimulus-responsive manner. However, the CDs-SA gel did not exhibit a color change as obvious as that observed for the CDs; consequently, some further modifications are still required to produce the optimal CDs-polysaccharides gel and realize that the color changes can be distinguished by the eye alone. This study not only realized a dual-function synergetic actuator that relies only on CDs as response sites, but also provides promising prospects for wireless multifunctional actuators.

Yingge Shi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xuyuan Yang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yongqiang Zhang: Investigation. Siyu Lu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 52122308). We would like to thank Editage (www.editage.cn) for English language editing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2024.123112.
